unit.name: Attention maps and the abstract attention operator
unit.description: Shows how attention weights can be visualized to interpret learned alignments, then abstracts attention away from RNNs into a stand-alone query/data/output operator.
unit.summary: The lecture next emphasizes that attention weights are not only useful for computation but also for interpretation, since the predicted attention matrix shows what the network is looking at while generating each output token [ts=1272s][ts=1287s]. In the English-to-French example, diagonal patterns indicate roughly in-order word correspondences, while off-diagonal or block patterns reveal reordering and less clean many-to-many alignments [ts=1401s][ts=1516s]. The instructor then extracts the common structure hidden inside the RNN example: there are data vectors containing information to summarize, query vectors representing outputs to compute, and attention returns one output vector per query by comparing the query to all data vectors, softmax-normalizing the scores, and taking a weighted combination of the data [ts=1587s][ts=1716s][ts=1814s][ts=1846s]. This reframing is the bridge from “attention in an RNN” to “attention as a general neural-network layer.”

key_points.text: Attention weights can be visualized as a 2D grid to inspect what the model attends to during translation.
Diagonal attention patterns indicate mostly in-order correspondence between input and output words.
Off-diagonal or block patterns reveal reordering or non one-to-one alignments between source and target phrases.
Abstractly, attention takes a query vector, looks back over data vectors, and produces an output vector summarizing the data for that query.
Single-query attention still follows the same pipeline: similarities, softmax attention weights, then a weighted combination of data vectors.

kp_catalog.name: Attention heatmaps as learned alignments
kp_catalog.description: Visualizing attention matrices can reveal approximate word alignments, reorderings, and partial many-to-many correspondences learned without explicit alignment supervision.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because attention heatmaps as learned alignments clarifies visualizing attention matrices can reveal approximate word alignments, reorderings, and partial many-to-many correspondences learned without explicit alignment supervision, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Attention as a query-to-data operator
kp_catalog.description: Abstractly, attention maps query vectors and data vectors to output vectors by letting each query retrieve a custom summary of the data.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because attention as a query-to-data operator formalizes how abstractly, attention maps query vectors and data vectors to output vectors by letting each query retrieve a custom summary of the data, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: Attention scoring and alignment weights
kp_catalog.description: Attention begins by comparing a decoder/query state with each candidate source state, then applying softmax to turn those scores into normalized attention weights. Attention scores can be computed with dot-product, bilinear/multiplicative, low-rank projected dot-product, or additive neural-net scoring.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because attention scoring and alignment weights clarifies attention begins by comparing a decoder/query state with each candidate source state, then applying softmax to turn those scores into normalized attention weights, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices though importance labels split between critical and high.
